skill.name: mcp-builder-ms
skill.description: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP), Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK), or C#/.NET (Microsoft MCP SDK).
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide for building MCP servers that enable LLMs to interact with external APIs and services.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation-only skill containing markdown guidance for building MCP servers. All 70 static findings are false positives: shell command examples (npm, python) are instructional references, URLs point to legitimate documentation sites, and filesystem references are relative path examples. No executable code or security risks present.

content.value_statement: Create Model Context Protocol servers that enable Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to interact with external services through well-designed tools. This guide covers Python, TypeScript, and C# implementations.
actual_capabilities: Guide users through creating MCP servers in Python, TypeScript, or C#/.NET
Provide best practices for tool naming, error handling, and response formatting
Explain transport selection (Streamable HTTP vs stdio) for different use cases
Reference Microsoft Azure MCP ecosystem and 48+ Azure service integrations
Outline 4-phase development process from research to evaluation
Link to official MCP protocol documentation and SDK resources
limitations: This is a guide skill, not executable code - it provides instructions, not implementation
Does not include working MCP server implementations - users must build their own
Does not provide authentication credentials or API keys for external services
Users need programming knowledge in Python, TypeScript, or C# to follow the guide

use_cases.title: Build Custom API Integration
use_cases.description: Create an MCP server to connect Claude with your company's internal APIs, enabling the AI to interact with your business systems.

use_cases.title: Extend Azure Services
use_cases.description: Use the guide to build MCP servers that integrate with Azure services beyond what the official Azure MCP Server provides.

use_cases.title: Create Third-Party Connectors
use_cases.description: Build MCP servers to connect Claude with SaaS platforms like GitHub, Slack, or custom third-party services.

prompt_templates.title: Getting Started with MCP
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to build an MCP server to connect Claude with [describe your API or service]. Walk me through the process from start to finish.

prompt_templates.title: Choosing Technology Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Should I use Python (FastMCP), TypeScript, or C#/.NET for my MCP server that needs to [describe functionality]? What are the trade-offs?

prompt_templates.title: Implementing Tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to implement a tool in my MCP server that [describe tool purpose]. Include input schema, output schema, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Testing the Server
prompt_templates.prompt: What is the process for testing my MCP server? How do I use MCP Inspector and create evaluations?

output_examples.input: I need to connect Claude with our internal REST API. Where do I start?
output_examples.output: Start with Phase 1: Research and Planning. First, understand the MCP protocol by reviewing the specification at modelcontextprotocol.io. Then identify your API endpoints, authentication requirements, and choose your implementation language (TypeScript recommended for broad compatibility). Set up your project structure and implement core infrastructure including API client, error handling, and response formatting.

output_examples.input: What language should I use for my MCP server?
output_examples.output: TypeScript is recommended for general MCP servers due to broad compatibility. Use Python (FastMCP) for data/ML pipelines with FastAPI integration. Choose C#/.NET if you are working primarily in the Azure/Microsoft ecosystem and need enterprise features. Each has SDKs: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk (TypeScript), mcp/FastMCP (Python), Microsoft.Mcp.Core (C#).

best_practices: Prioritize comprehensive API coverage over workflow tools to give agents flexibility
Use clear, descriptive tool names with consistent prefixes like github_create_issue
Design actionable error messages that guide agents toward solutions
anti_patterns: Do not skip Phase 4 (evaluation) - testing with real questions is essential
Avoid returning unstructured text when structured data would be more useful
Do not ignore transport selection - choose stdio for local, HTTP for remote

faq.question: What is MCP?
faq.answer: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a standard protocol that enables LLMs like Claude to interact with external services through well-designed tools. It provides a consistent way to expose APIs and services to AI assistants.

faq.question: Do I need to build my own MCP server?
faq.answer: Check if Microsoft already provides what you need. Azure MCP Server covers 48+ Azure services, and Foundry MCP handles AI Foundry agents and evals. Build custom only if integrating with third-party or internal APIs.

faq.question: Which language should I use?
faq.answer: TypeScript is recommended for general use due to broad compatibility. Python is ideal for data/ML workflows. C#/.NET is best for Azure/Microsoft ecosystem integration.

faq.question: How do I test my MCP server?
faq.answer: Use MCP Inspector (npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector) for interactive testing. Then create evaluation questions to test whether LLMs can effectively use your server for real tasks.

faq.question: What transport should I use?
faq.answer: Use stdio for local servers, desktop apps, and single-user scenarios. Use Streamable HTTP for remote servers, multi-tenant services, and Agent Service deployments.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No, this is a documentation and guidance skill. It provides instructions and references to help you build your own MCP server. It does not contain executable code.
